nativeRange.habitat: Still or slow-moving freshwater bodies including ponds, lakes, ditches and slow rivers; tolerates a wide temperature range from cool temperate to tropical

decorPreferences: open surface area for floating growth
optional anchor points or weights if used as a background plant

goodWith: cold-water and pond fish such as goldfish
avoidWith: strongly herbivorous fish such as silver dollars or large plecos
very high-flow tanks that shred the brittle stems

summary: Hornwort is one of the hardiest, fastest-growing rootless stem plants available for freshwater aquariums. Ceratophyllum demersum grows well floating or loosely anchored and tolerates a wide range of conditions, from cool unheated tanks to tropical aquariums. It is widely reported to suppress algae by competing for nutrients and releasing natural inhibitory compounds.
careNotes: Hornwort (Ceratophyllum demersum) is a cosmopolitan rootless aquatic plant and one of the most forgiving options for freshwater aquariums and ponds. Because it has no true roots, it is most commonly floated at the surface where access to light is best, but it can also be loosely tied to driftwood, weighted down, or pushed into the substrate as a background plant, where it forms a dense, feathery underwater bush. Growth is very fast — commonly 3–10 cm per week under good light — and stems can reach 30–80 cm in length under typical aquarium conditions. The plant is brittle and tends to shed its needle-like leaves when first acclimating to a new tank, when handled roughly, or when exposed to sudden water changes; this shedding is normal and generally stops once the plant settles in. Regular trimming is important to keep growth in check and to prevent the plant from blanketing the surface and shading other plants lower in the tank.
breedingNotes: Propagation is straightforward: cut a side shoot or trim a stem section and let it float, anchor, or replant — any segment will quickly grow into a new plant. Stem cutting and fragmentation are the most common methods used in home aquariums, as the plant rarely flowers or sets seed in captivity.

primary.name: Tropica
primary.notes: Primary source. The Tropica search landing page (the URL supplied by the controller) returns navigation only; the actual species page was located via search and used here. Tropica entry covers the 021A clone and provides light/CO2 demand, growth rate, height and care level but no specific water-parameter numbers.

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature range, floating/anchored placement, rootless biology, algicidal nutrient-competition effect and fry-cover benefit.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Ceratophyllaceae, order Ceratophyllales), cosmopolitan native range, temperature range (4–30 °C; 10–30 °C optimal), pH 6–9, kH 5–24, growth rate, propagation, and explicit confirmation that the plant 'produces a natural algae growth inhibitor'.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature range (10–30 °C), beginner care level, very-fast growth (3–10 cm/week), floating preference, easy propagation by trimming, cold-tolerance for unheated tanks and outdoor ponds. Substituted for Buce Plant which returned 404 for this species.
